title: Trump Sends Witkoff/Kushner to Islamabad, Issues 'NO MORE MR. NICE GUY' Infrastructure Ultimatum
detail: President Trump announced on April 19 (Day 51) that a US government delegation — including Special Envoy Steve Witkoff and senior adviser Jared Kushner — was traveling to Islamabad, Pakistan for negotiations with Iran, expected to arrive Monday evening (April 20) with talks scheduled for Tuesday April 22, the same day the ceasefire expires. Simultaneously, Trump issued the most severe ultimatum of the conflict, posting: 'If they don't make a DEAL, the United States is going to knock out every single Power Plant, and every single Bridge, in Iran. NO MORE MR. NICE GUY!' Energy Secretary Chris Wright warned that transiting the Strait of Hormuz poses active safety risks. Trump cited Iranian gunboat fire on merchant vessels as a 'serious violation' of the ceasefire agreement. Trump's approval rating stood at 37%, with 66% of Americans disapproving of his Iran war handling.

media.caption: Trump sends Witkoff/Kushner to Islamabad talks as Iran ceasefire deadline looms April 22

title: IRGC Fires on Indian-Flagged Tankers in Hormuz; India Summons Iran Ambassador
detail: Iranian IRGC gunboats fired on Indian-flagged crude oil tankers attempting transit of the Strait of Hormuz on April 18–19, forcing the vessels to reverse course. India summoned Iran's ambassador in protest. The IRGC issued a formal warning that 'approaching the Strait of Hormuz will be considered cooperation with the enemy, and the violating vessel will be targeted.' Trump cited the shootings on April 19 as a 'serious violation' and a 'total violation of our ceasefire agreement,' using the incidents to justify his infrastructure destruction ultimatum. At least two Indian-flagged tankers were turned back at gunpoint. The IRGC maintained a declared closure of the strait, with all non-IRGC-authorized vessels refused passage.

media.caption: IRGC gunboats enforce Strait of Hormuz closure, firing on Indian-flagged tankers

title: Iran's Supreme Leader Vows IRGC Navy Will Inflict 'New Defeats' on US and Israel
detail: Iran's Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei issued a statement on April 19 declaring the IRGC navy was 'ready to make the enemies taste the bitterness of new defeats,' pledging the naval forces would inflict 'new defeats' on the US and Israel in the Strait of Hormuz and Persian Gulf. The statement came hours before Trump's escalatory infrastructure ultimatum and coincided with Islamabad security preparations — Pakistani authorities closed roads and set up checkpoints around the Marriott and Serena hotels in advance of the US delegation's arrival. The statement was released while the IRGC Navy was actively enforcing the Hormuz closure by firing on Indian-flagged tankers.

media.caption: Iran's supreme leader vows IRGC navy will deliver 'new defeats' as Hormuz remains closed

title: Ceasefire Expires April 22 With 'No Deal in Sight'; Ghalibaf Confirms 'Far Off'
detail: The two-week US-Iran ceasefire (which began April 8) is set to expire on April 22 with no formal peace deal in place. Iran's chief negotiator Ghalibaf acknowledged 'many gaps and some fundamental points remain' and that 'the distance to a final agreement remains considerable,' citing 'distrust of the United States' as a fundamental obstacle. NPR reported the ceasefire is expiring with 'no deal in sight.' The core sticking points: the US demands a permanent end to all uranium enrichment and removal of Iran's highly enriched uranium stockpile; Iran has offered only a 3-to-5-year enrichment freeze versus the US demand of 20 years. Iran stated publicly 'no date has been set for US talks,' contradicting signals from the Trump administration. Pakistan's FM Dar was actively shuttling between capitals to bridge the gap ahead of the April 22 deadline.

media.caption: US-Iran ceasefire set to expire April 22 with no deal in sight as talks stall
